question:
Determine all real numbers $a > 0$ for which there exists a nonnegative
continuous function $f(x)$ defined on $[0,a]$ with the property that the
region
\[
R = \{ (x,y) ; 0
\le x \le a, 0 \le y \le
f(x) \}
\]
has perimeter $k$ units and area $k$ square units for some real number $k$.
solution:
The answer is $\{a\,|\,a>2\}$. If $a>2$, then the function $f(x) =
2a/(a-2)$ has the desired property; both perimeter and area of $R$
in this case are $2a^2/(a-2)$. Now suppose that $a\leq 2$, and let
$f(x)$ be a nonnegative continuous function on $[0,a]$. Let
$P=(x_0,y_0)$ be a point on the graph of $f(x)$ with maximal
$y$-coordinate; then the area of $R$ is at most $ay_0$ since it lies
below the line $y=y_0$. On the other hand, the points $(0,0)$,
$(a,0)$, and $P$ divide the boundary of $R$ into three sections. The
length of the section between $(0,0)$ and $P$ is at least the
distance between $(0,0)$ and $P$, which is at least $y_0$; the
length of the section between $P$ and $(a,0)$ is similarly at least
$y_0$; and the length of the section between $(0,0)$ and $(a,0)$ is
$a$. Since $a\leq 2$, we have $2y_0 + a > ay_0$ and hence the
perimeter of $R$ is strictly greater than the area of $R$.

descriptive_long.question:
Determine all real numbers $lengthab > 0$ for which there exists a nonnegative continuous function $funcvar(inputxs)$ defined on $[0,lengthab]$ with the property that the region
\[
regionab = \{ (inputxs,outputys) ; 0
\le inputxs \le lengthab, 0 \le outputys \le
funcvar(inputxs) \}
\]
has perimeter $measurek$ units and area $measurek$ square units for some real number $measurek$.
descriptive_long.solution:
The answer is $\{lengthab\,|\,lengthab>2\}$. If $lengthab>2$, then the function $funcvar(inputxs) =
2lengthab/(lengthab-2)$ has the desired property; both perimeter and area of $regionab$
in this case are $2lengthab^2/(lengthab-2)$. Now suppose that $lengthab\leq 2$, and let
$funcvar(inputxs)$ be a nonnegative continuous function on $[0,lengthab]$. Let
$pointpt=(inputzero,outputzero)$ be a point on the graph of $funcvar(inputxs)$ with maximal
$outputys$-coordinate; then the area of $regionab$ is at most $lengthab\,outputzero$ since it lies
below the line $outputys=outputzero$. On the other hand, the points $(0,0)$,
$(lengthab,0)$, and $pointpt$ divide the boundary of $regionab$ into three sections. The
length of the section between $(0,0)$ and $pointpt$ is at least the
distance between $(0,0)$ and $pointpt$, which is at least $outputzero$; the
length of the section between $pointpt$ and $(lengthab,0)$ is similarly at least
$outputzero$; and the length of the section between $(0,0)$ and $(lengthab,0)$ is
$lengthab$. Since $lengthab\leq 2$, we have $2outputzero + lengthab > lengthab\,outputzero$ and hence the
perimeter of $regionab$ is strictly greater than the area of $regionab$.

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
Determine all real numbers $horsehair > 0$ for which there exists a nonnegative
continuous function $blueberry(lemonade)$ defined on $[0,horsehair]$ with the property that the
region
\[
seashells = \{ (lemonade,honeycomb) ; 0
\le lemonade \le horsehair, 0 \le honeycomb \le
blueberry(lemonade) \}
\]
has perimeter $driftwood$ units and area $driftwood$ square units for some real number $driftwood$.
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
The answer is $\{horsehair\,|\,horsehair>2\}$. If $horsehair>2$, then the function $blueberry(lemonade) =
2horsehair/(horsehair-2)$ has the desired property; both perimeter and area of $seashells$
in this case are $2horsehair^2/(horsehair-2)$. Now suppose that $horsehair\leq 2$, and let
$blueberry(lemonade)$ be a nonnegative continuous function on $[0,horsehair]$. Let
$acornleaf=(peppermint,candlewax)$ be a point on the graph of $blueberry(lemonade)$ with maximal
honeycomb-coordinate; then the area of $seashells$ is at most $horsehaircandlewax$ since it lies
below the line $honeycomb=candlewax$. On the other hand, the points $(0,0)$,
$(horsehair,0)$, and $acornleaf$ divide the boundary of $seashells$ into three sections. The
length of the section between $(0,0)$ and $acornleaf$ is at least the
distance between $(0,0)$ and $acornleaf$, which is at least $candlewax$; the
length of the section between $acornleaf$ and $(horsehair,0)$ is similarly at least
$candlewax$; and the length of the section between $(0,0)$ and $(horsehair,0)$ is
$horsehair$. Since $horsehair\leq 2$, we have $2candlewax + horsehair > horsehaircandlewax$ and hence the
perimeter of $seashells$ is strictly greater than the area of $seashells$.

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
Determine all real numbers $negativespan > 0$ for which there exists a nonnegative
continuous function $discretefun(verticalvar)$ defined on $[0,negativespan]$ with the property that the
region
\[
singlepoint = \{ (verticalvar,horizontalvar) ; 0
\le verticalvar \le negativespan, 0 \le horizontalvar \le
discretefun(verticalvar) \}
\]
has perimeter $variablerate$ units and area $variablerate$ square units for some real number $variablerate$.
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
The answer is $\{negativespan\,|\,negativespan>2\}$. If $negativespan>2$, then the function $discretefun(verticalvar) =
2negativespan/(negativespan-2)$ has the desired property; both perimeter and area of $singlepoint$
in this case are $2negativespan^2/(negativespan-2)$. Now suppose that $negativespan\leq 2$, and let
$discretefun(verticalvar)$ be a nonnegative continuous function on $[0,negativespan]$. Let
$lineobject=(verticalorigin,horizontalorigin)$ be a point on the graph of $discretefun(verticalvar)$ with maximal
horizontalvar-coordinate; then the area of $singlepoint$ is at most $negativespan horizontalorigin$ since it lies
below the line $horizontalvar=horizontalorigin$. On the other hand, the points $(0,0)$,
$(negativespan,0)$, and $lineobject$ divide the boundary of $singlepoint$ into three sections. The
length of the section between $(0,0)$ and $lineobject$ is at least the
distance between $(0,0)$ and $lineobject$, which is at least $horizontalorigin$; the
length of the section between $lineobject$ and $(negativespan,0)$ is similarly at least
$horizontalorigin$; and the length of the section between $(0,0)$ and $(negativespan,0)$ is
$negativespan$. Since $negativespan\leq 2$, we have $2horizontalorigin + negativespan > negativespan horizontalorigin$ and hence the
perimeter of $singlepoint$ is strictly greater than the area of $singlepoint$.

garbled_string.question:
Determine all real numbers $sebtundq > 0$ for which there exists a nonnegative
continuous function $qzxwvtnp(drlqspow)$ defined on $[0,sebtundq]$ with the property that the
region
\[
vmnckefu = \{ (drlqspow,orhtcvae) ; 0
\le drlqspow \le sebtundq, 0 \le orhtcvae \le
qzxwvtnp(drlqspow) \}
\]
has perimeter $pwrxalio$ units and area $pwrxalio$ square units for some real number $pwrxalio$.
garbled_string.solution:
The answer is $\{sebtundq\,|\,sebtundq>2\}$. If $sebtundq>2$, then the function $qzxwvtnp(drlqspow) =
2sebtundq/(sebtundq-2)$ has the desired property; both perimeter and area of $vmnckefu$
in this case are $2sebtundq^2/(sebtundq-2)$. Now suppose that $sebtundq\leq 2$, and let
$qzxwvtnp(drlqspow)$ be a nonnegative continuous function on $[0,sebtundq]$. Let
$hjgrksla=(fgewlupa,zkpmodri)$ be a point on the graph of $qzxwvtnp(drlqspow)$ with maximal
$orhtcvae$-coordinate; then the area of $vmnckefu$ is at most $sebtundq\,zkpmodri$ since it lies
below the line $orhtcvae=zkpmodri$. On the other hand, the points $(0,0)$,
$(sebtundq,0)$, and $hjgrksla$ divide the boundary of $vmnckefu$ into three sections. The
length of the section between $(0,0)$ and $hjgrksla$ is at least the
distance between $(0,0)$ and $hjgrksla$, which is at least $zkpmodri$; the
length of the section between $hjgrksla$ and $(sebtundq,0)$ is similarly at least
$zkpmodri$; and the length of the section between $(0,0)$ and $(sebtundq,0)$ is
$sebtundq$. Since $sebtundq\leq 2$, we have $2zkpmodri + sebtundq > sebtundq\,zkpmodri$ and hence the
perimeter of $vmnckefu$ is strictly greater than the area of $vmnckefu$.

kernel_variant.question:
For which positive real numbers \(L\) does there exist a non-negative continuous function \(g:[0,L]\to\mathbb R\) such that the planar region
\[
D=\{(x,y):0\le x\le L,\;0\le y\le g(x)\}
\]
has its area equal to its perimeter (both equal to some real value \(p>0\))?
kernel_variant.solution:
Answer: Such a function exists if and only if L > 2.

Proof.

1. Existence when L > 2.
Choose the constant function g(x) \equiv  h on [0, L], so D is the rectangle of width L and height h.  Its area is
  A = L\cdot h,
and its perimeter is
  P = 2(L + h).
We require A = P, i.e.
  Lh = 2(L + h).
Solving,
  Lh - 2h = 2L,
  h (L - 2) = 2L,
  h = 2L/(L - 2).
Since L > 2 this h > 0.  Then indeed
  A = Lh = 2L^2/(L - 2),
  P = 2(L + h) = 2L + 2\cdot (2L/(L - 2)) = 2L^2/(L - 2)
also, so A = P as desired.  Thus for every L > 2 we have a continuous nonnegative g with Area = Perimeter.

2. Non-existence when 0 < L \leq  2.
Suppose to the contrary that some nonnegative continuous g on [0, L] makes Area(D) = Perimeter(D) = p > 0.
Let y_0 = max_{x\in [0,L]} g(x).  Then every point of D lies at or below y = y_0, so
  Area(D) \leq  L\cdot y_0.                                         (1)
Next, trace \partial D from (0,0) to (L,0) up along the graph of g(x).  The two ``upper'' arcs---from (0,0) to the highest point P=(x_0,y_0), and from P back down to (L,0)---each have length at least the straight-line distance from P to the corresponding base corner, hence each \geq  y_0.  The base segment has length L.  Thus
  Perimeter(D) \geq  L + 2y_0.                                 (2)
Subtracting (1) from (2) gives
  Perimeter(D) - Area(D) \geq  [L + 2y_0] - [L y_0]
                             = L + (2 - L)\cdot y_0.
Since L > 0 and y_0 \geq  0, we have L + (2 - L)\cdot y_0 \geq  L > 0 when L \leq  2.  Hence Perimeter(D) - Area(D) > 0, contradicting the assumption Area = Perimeter.  Therefore no such g can exist if L \leq  2.

3. Conclusion.
Putting (1) and (2) together, we find exactly those L > 2 admit a nonnegative continuous g:[0,L]\to \mathbb{R} with the property that Area(D) = Perimeter(D).  Thus the answer is
  { L \in  \mathbb{R} : L > 2 }.

core_steps: Pick a rectangle of width a: solve 2(a+h)=a·h ⇒ h=2a/(a−2) to exhibit equality when a>2.
Compute its area (a·h) and perimeter (2(a+h)) to confirm they are identical.
For an arbitrary non-negative continuous f, let y₀=max f; then Area ≤ a·y₀.
Observe  a≤2 ⇒ a+2y₀ > a·y₀, giving Perimeter>Area, contradiction; hence only a>2 works.

slot1.description: Symbol used for the interval length (currently 'a') — any single letter could be used without affecting the logic.

slot2.description: Symbol used for the common numerical value of area and perimeter (currently 'k') — freely replaceable.
